title: Perception differences between Japan and the West
display_title: Perception differences between Japan and the West
wikitext:
[]: Perception differences between Japan and the West             Quote Post by The Imperialist » Tue Oct 23, 2007 3:22 pm For now, I would say there aren't any differences. (Somebody says in the background: ‘Then why the hell are you making this post for?’) Well, one reason is that ObsessiveMathfreak (I think it is spelled this way) encouraged me a bit, second of all, I am Japanese, and I would like to hear opinions which were never thought of before in Japan. However, I will now make my first state notwithstanding as first of all, my ‘On Wagner’ thread originated from this Classical music society (sob, it doesn’t exist anymore), and a few Wagnerites like myself, were doing this research on implementations of Wagner’s operatic works, (storyline, leitmotifs, Gesamtkunstwerk etc.) into later media works. That is the great moment in history when I took up Evangelion, (list of characters of Evangelion to the Ring Cycle could be seen in the ‘On Wagner’ thread note: incompleted), when I was doing some looking up on anime/manga works with Wagnerian influences, I came across Evangelion, and here you have another convert (actually, 12 others I think). Secondly, Evangelion seems to be criticising the current political system of the Japanese Government, as all the官僚 (the bureaucrats) are the ones in power behind the scenes (mirroring SEELE, who have disillusionment in the welfare of Japan--> SEELE’s original plan for Instrumentality), and the events in EoE metaphorically portraying the eventual fall of Japan, (and now the Wagnerian part), if we are true to ourselves, taking back the original Japanese mentality lost after WWII (thinking about your true self to return to human form from LCL), we can rebuild ourselves. Well, what is this ‘mentality’ lost? Well, it is not individualism (個人主義), but for the Japanese national to grasp back the ideals of the 公 (ōyake) (it is kind of hard to explain as it is a very unique way of thinking, but basically, it is the respect for others, that above the individual is the public). It is disputed what represents the mentality, but here are a few parallels: Evas+Eva pilots: The ones resisting the onslaught of individualistic egoism or more specifically the prediction of the coming ‘messiah’ politician of Japan to save it from the fall, also the younger generation who are sensing that the present situation is spelling doom for Japan. Angels: Foreign invasion/negative influences SEELE: the bureaucrats NERV: the current social revolutionaries who have failed many times but deserves credit. JSSDF (in EoE): corruption, people wanting the status quo controlled by the kanryo. Gendo: The ultra-nationalist, where they are succeeding up to a point, but their radicalism will spell their downfall. And a few others, but it will be too tiresome to mention.

[]:             Quote Post by NAveryW » Tue Oct 23, 2007 3:56 pm I believe Anno said that Evangelion was partially made as a statement on the current (at the time, and possibly still) way Japanese society thinks of/uses adolescents... I don't quite remember how, though. Something about the school structure and social... stuff... I think.

[]:             Quote Post by The Imperialist » Tue Oct 23, 2007 3:57 pm Well, it is definitely about the rotten political and social structure of Japan... not sure how the school bit fits in... but yes, it is still ongoing.

[]:             Quote Post by NAveryW » Tue Oct 23, 2007 4:05 pm You can read about Anno's view of Japan in the editorial article "Let's Die Together". It seems to be largely negative, and he considers Japan to be a "nation of children".

[]:             Quote Post by Squiggy » Tue Oct 23, 2007 4:11 pm NAveryW wrote: You can read about Anno's view of Japan in the editorial article "Let's Die Together". It seems to be largely negative, and he considers Japan to be a "nation of children".

[]:             Quote Post by The Imperialist » Tue Oct 23, 2007 4:17 pm Any URL? ANd also, america is basically the big brother of Japan.

[]:             Quote Post by Squiggy » Tue Oct 23, 2007 4:24 pm The Imperialist wrote: Any URL? ANd also, america is basically the big brother of Japan.

[]:             Quote Post by The Imperialist » Tue Oct 23, 2007 4:56 pm Returning to the original purpose of this post, I will say that the anime is kind of used as a political protest against the government, and a plea to the new younger generation to stand up and do what is right for the future of Japan.

[]:             Quote Post by BobBQ » Tue Oct 23, 2007 5:00 pm Hm... Out of curiosity, what's your take on the uyoku dantai?

[]:             Quote Post by The Imperialist » Tue Oct 23, 2007 5:21 pm Well, I believe they are better than the sayoku, because in my eyes, the sayoku are masochists who keeps on thinking Japan is the worst etc. etc., and for some reason wants to hamper Japan's progress. I do have to say that the uyoku dantai are sligtly extreme, (well depends which group), but I do agree on their ideas about a change of mentality in Japanese people, renewed pride for Japan, instead of being ashamed of it. One thing I couldn't really forgive the uyoku is that they complete ruined the national anthem by keeping on broadcasting them in the streets (when I was still in Japan), and it was horribly annoying. I myself is the Forward-Conservatism (don't know the real name, as many say things like neo-conservatism, but you know what I mean), which is to respect the ways of shinto, bushido, and our ancesters, and using that virtue for the future of Japan in general.

[]: Re: Perception differences between Japan and the West             Quote Post by ObsessiveMathsFreak » Tue Oct 23, 2007 6:56 pm The Imperialist wrote: Evas+Eva pilots: The ones resisting the onslaught of individualistic egoism or more specifically the prediction of the coming ‘messiah’ politician of Japan to save it from the fall, also the younger generation who are sensing that the present situation is spelling doom for Japan.

[]:             Quote Post by EvaCub » Tue Oct 23, 2007 8:33 pm The Imperialist wrote: Returning to the original purpose of this post, I will say that the anime is kind of used as a political protest against the government, and a plea to the new younger generation to stand up and do what is right for the future of Japan.

[]:             Quote Post by chee » Wed Oct 24, 2007 12:07 am Patlabor 2 gave me a similar vibe.

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Wed Oct 24, 2007 6:28 am By 2006, Japanese population has already peaked as the demographic transition continues. Sentiments like in NGE, a decade earlier, would be picking up on already extant trends.

[]: Re: Perception differences between Japan and the West             Quote Post by The Imperialist » Wed Oct 24, 2007 10:40 am ObsessiveMathsFreak wrote: This isn't resonating with me at all. Gendo seems a bit too dry and cynical to qualify as a zealous patriot.

[]:             Quote Post by chee » Wed Oct 24, 2007 1:57 pm It only makes sense that Anno would go on to make a politicially-minded work, considering whose protege we once was. (HINT: His name starts with a "H" and ends with an "ayao Miyazaki".)

[]:             Quote Post by The Imperialist » Wed Oct 24, 2007 2:32 pm Jawohl. Ich stimme.

[]:             Quote Post by chee » Wed Oct 24, 2007 2:48 pm Don't forget who his fellow protege was either- Mamoru "Kerberos" Oshii. (Thus, I explain the entire joke behind The Con Where Anime Died to those who didn't get it.)

[]:             Quote Post by The Imperialist » Wed Oct 24, 2007 2:50 pm Ich stimme auch.

[]:             Quote Post by chee » Wed Oct 24, 2007 2:55 pm Oh yeah? Well...erm....Ich....uh....Ich wandte mich.....m-mich....mich und sah.....screw it. EDIT: Ha HA! "Ich wandte mich, und sah an alles Unrecht das geschah unter der sonne"!!!111 Anyway, getting back on topic, I find it interesting that NGE is considered a politically engaged work. Over here, from what I can tell, it's viewed as more of an existential work.
